coredata.dc:title: Temperature dependence of interface barrier height change as implicated by field emission studies of aligned-multiwall carbon nanotubes
coredata.prism:copyright: Copyright © 2003 Elsevier Science B.V. All rights reserved.
coredata.dc:creator: Chen, Sheng-Yuan
Lue, Juh-Tzeng
coredata.dc:description: We have observed that the change of field emission currents of multiwalled nanotube (MWNT) is not sensitive but detectable to temperature variation within the range from 300 K to 20 K. However, the characteristic curve cannot be congruently fitted by Fowler–Nordheim (FN) theory. Assuming a semiconducting amorphous carbon dot grown on the tube end, the dominated field emission mechanism will be the thermal electron tunneling caused by the high aspect ratio of the tube. This analysis conclusively addresses that the electron affinity and the energy gap of the caps are not changed while the interface barrier height increases as temperature increases.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Multiwalled carbon nanotube
Field emission

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) The SEM image of aligned MWNT, and (b) the tube top end is closed and covered by amorphous carbon nanoparticles.
(a) The temperature dependence of field emission for aligned MWNT films, and (b) the demonstration of fitting results for 300 K by thermionic equation.

The theoretical fitting for aligned MWNT films with χ the electron affinity, E g the energy gap of caps or nanoparticles, E cs−E f the energy difference between the surface conduction level and the bulk Fermi level. The fitting parameters are a1=1.21×102 Acm −2
K −2 , b=6.83×107 Vcm −1 eV −3/2 , a2=1.56×10−6 eVAV −2 .
Schematic energy band diagrams for closed MWNTs at (a) high temperatures, and (b) low temperatures.

head.ce:title: Temperature dependence of interface barrier height change as implicated by field emission studies of aligned-multiwall carbon nanotubes

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Electrical Engineering, National Tsing Hua University, Hsinchu 30042, Taiwan, ROC

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Physics, National Tsing Hua University, Hsinchu 30042, Taiwan, ROC

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: We have observed that the change of field emission currents of multiwalled nanotube (MWNT) is not sensitive but detectable to temperature variation within the range from 300 K to 20 K. However, the characteristic curve cannot be congruently fitted by Fowler–Nordheim (FN) theory. Assuming a semiconducting amorphous carbon dot grown on the tube end, the dominated field emission mechanism will be the thermal electron tunneling caused by the high aspect ratio of the tube. This analysis conclusively addresses that the electron affinity and the energy gap of the caps are not changed while the interface barrier height increases as temperature increases.

ce:section.ce:para: The key problem in the development of field emission displays (FED) is to fabricate effective cold cathodes providing intense electron current at room temperature. Carbon materials like diamond and diamond-like films are the most prominent material, because of their possible unique electronic property—negative electron affinity (NEA). Carbon nanotube (CNT), a new stable form of carbon, consists of one or several bundled graphitic tubes [1]. Due to its unique geometry and electronic properties, CNT has attracted substantial attention as electron field emission sources [2,3]. Field emission (FE) from CNT with high current densities at low applied electric fields has been demonstrated by several groups [4–6].

Despite of the high aspect ratio (h/r, where r is the average radius and h is the height of the tubes, respectively) of CNT, the FE mechanism, which causes the excellent FE properties, is very fascinated. Some authors indicated that multiwalled nanotubes (MWNTs) have very low work function (0.5–2 eV) as derived from the Fowler–Nordheim (FN) plot [7,8], and others concluded that CNTs behave as metallic emitters [9–11]. Obraztsov et al. [12] proposed that the high efficiency of FE is explained in terms of re-hybridization of inter atomic bonds from sp2 to sp3 for atoms located at the bends of graphite sheets [8,12,13]. In general, most field emission studies on CNT implement the FN equation to model the current–voltage (I–V) characteristic. However, the FN equation was derived for electron emission from pure metallic emitter at low temperatures and high fields, and disregards the temperature effect. In some recent reports [6,14,15] illustrated that the FN model is not sufficient to quantitatively characterize the emission. A failure of the FN model in describing I–V data may be caused by nonmetallic emitters, tetrahedral amorphous carbon coatings, or the localize states at the tips [16–18]. A thermionic emission current from a metal–semiconductor Schottky diode [19,20] is expected to be more germane to address the I–V field emission of CNT.

In this Letter, we report the temperature dependency FE results from aligned closed multiwalled carbon nanotube (MWNT) fabricated by a microwave (MW)/radio frequency (RF) assisted hot filament chemical vapor deposition (HFCVD). The data were analyzed by the thermionic model [21,22] and compared with the FN model.

In this study, a thin nickel or an alloy film composed of nickel and iron was coated on silicon (Si) substrates by thermal evaporator to play the role of catalysis. The thickness of metal films was about 20–30 nm. Prior to CNT growth, the nickel film was oxidized at 1000° for 30 min in air to spread into nanometer grains. The substrate was then transferred to a home-made MW/RF assisted hot filament chemical vapor deposition (HFCVD) chamber which was pumped down to 10−3 torr. Methane (CH4) and hydrogen (H2) gas were used as the carbon source and diluted gas with flow rates to be 60 sccm and 80 sccm, respectively. As the working pressure of 10 torr had been stabilized, the MW power for pre-ionization was turned on. The tungsten filament was heated to ∼1700°C while the substrates located underneath the filament were kept at temperatures near 600–700°C depending on their distance with the filament A self-bias of about −150 V induced by the RF plasma is crucial to grow aligned MWNTs which are perpendicular to the substrate. The growth time was 30 min after the filament temperature was stabilized.

Typically, a deposited film was dull dark in color and could be grown uniformly in 3×3 cm2 area. Scanning electron microscopy (SEM) was carried out to inspect the morphology and the microstructure of the grown CNTs. To study the temperature effect on the field emission, the samples were mounted on the cooling stage of a helium closed-cycle refrigerator. A diode structure was used to measure FE. The measured current–voltage (I–V) data were fitted to the model dwelled in Refs. [21,22] at the condition of low temperatures with the valence band E c being far different from the donor ionization level E d such as (E c−E d)/kT⪢1 that yields
(1) J total =a1T2e−θ/kTe
−bχ3/2
βF
+a2
(βF)2
χ+Eg
e− b(χ+Eg)3/2
βF
,
where F is the external electrical field, β is the field enhancement factor,
a1= qmk2
2π2ℏ3
,a2= q3
16π2ℏt2(y) ,
b= 4 2m
3qℏ ν(y),
ν(y) and t(y) are tabulated functions involving elliptic integrals, χ is the electron affinity, θ=E cs−E f is the difference energy between interface conduction band of a-carbon semiconductor and Fermi level of metallic carbon tubes.

Typical SEM images of the CNT film are demonstrated in Fig. 1 . It is clearly shown that the CNTs are vertically aligned to the substrate, and their distribution is uniform. We tacitly assumed that the effective emission area is the average of nanotubes and the bare open surface. The CNTs diameter is about 30–70 nm with a height of 8–9 μm. The film contains some carbonaceous nanoparticles deposited on the top of the film, which accordingly affect the FE behaviors.

To study the temperature effect on the field emission, the emission current was measured in cycles of cooling down and warming up. The dependence of current density on electric fields at various temperatures is depicted in Fig. 2(a) , with the fitting results demonstrated in Fig. 2(b). The inset in Fig. 2(a) is a logarithm scale of the y-axis. The curves illustrate that the emission currents insensitively depend on temperatures. This behavior is quite different from the temperature dependence of FE characteristic of diamond films or diamond-like (DLC) films [22,23], owing to the structure and strong field emission on the sharp tip of carbon nanotubes instead of a flat emitter.

The FE properties of MWNT films have a prosaic relation with temperatures. Many researches [7,8] claimed their successful fitting of the emission characteristic curve of CNT by adjusting the work function (ψ) of the FN equation and the field enhancement factor (β). Accordingly, the evaluated work function and emission factor deviate with an inconclusive large values between 0.42–3.55 eV and 41–738, respectively. This fact is controvertible to the real physical sense which convinces us that ψ and β should be inconspicious to temperature. Apparently a monotonic increasing curve can be readily fitted by any exponential or power-low functions by merely adjusting one variable, whereas the FN equation has been ubiquitously used with published works. Furthermore, even the best FN fitting cannot truly match the slowly variation of the experimental emission I–V curve as shown in Fig. 2(b). It is not surprised that some references [6,14,15] had shown that the FN equation is not sufficient to quantitatively characterize the emission.

In this Letter, the field enhancement factor β (=207) is established from the fitting data of FE at 300 K by Eq. (1). This β is fixed at various temperatures. The ideal β was determined from the average radius (r) and height (h) of the tubes. Theoretically, we can roughly estimate β∼400 (for r and h are 20 nm and 8 μm, respectively), which is higher than the simulated value 207. The plausible reason is that screening effect [24,25] from neighboring CNTs can reduce the value of β.

The thermionic equation involves three fundamental parameters of material properties, i.e., the electron affinity χ, the energy gap E g, and the interface barrier height E cs−E f. Fig. 3 is the theoretical fitting of Fig. 2(a), in which β was fixed at 207. It implies that the energy difference between the conduction band and the Fermi level (E cs−E f) decreases as temperature decreases, while the electron affinity χ and the energy gap E g (∼2.5 eV) is almost independent of temperatures. The tube body of MWNT is metallic behavior based on electrical transport measurements [26] that ensuing the defects contained in the aligned CNT bodies do not affect the FE behavior at the interface. A sophisticated assumption preludes that the work function for field emission may contribute from two terms. Firstly, it may arise from the electron affinity of caps which are n-type semiconducting amorphous carbon embodied with localized states [5,16,27]. The caps may contain sp3 bonds with a band gap of 1–3 eV [28] to provide FE currents. Secondly, it may come from the Schottky barrier height resulting from contact of semiconductor and metallic CNTs.

Thereby we can clearly illustrate the FE mechanism from aligned CNT films and elucidate the band diagram as shown in Fig. 4 . The electrons emit to vacuum by tunneling or thermal-tunneling process. The model is similar to metal–semiconductor Schottky contact. The contact barrier height [20,29] (ϕ Bn=E cs−E f) can be express as
(2) ϕ Bn = ε i
ε i +q2δD s
(ϕ m −χ)+ 1− ε i
ε i +q2δD s
E g
q −ϕ0
−Δϕ,
where D s is the surface density of state of semiconductor, χ is the electron affinity of semiconductor, ϕ 0 is the surface Fermi level, ϕ m is the work function of metal, ε i is the dielectric constant of insulator, δ is the thickness of insulator (δ≈0, in this case), E g is the energy gap of semiconductor, Δϕ is Schottky barrier lowering. The variation of ϕ m [30] with temperature is negligibly small in compared with the variation of barrier height. At low temperatures, few electrons flow out from the metallic MWNT to surface localized states due to less ionization of localized state. The Fermi level of interface localized states increases resulting in lowering down the interface barrier height as temperature decreases. The enhancement of emission current due to barrier lowering will be compensated by the reduction of tunneling currents as temperatures decrease alluding the flat response to temperature effect. Furthermore, the sharp tip of the carbon tubes implying a high electric field F (∼50000 V/cm) which overwhelms the temperature effect.

There were many studies on the fabrication and field emission characteristics of aligned CNT [12,31,32], but few researches focused on the field emission mechanisms. In this work, aligned multiwalled carbon nanotubes (MWNT) were fabricated by a microwave/radio frequency assisted hot-filament chemical vapor deposition (HFCVD). The experimental results indicate that the FE characteristics are insensitive to temperatures, which cannot be successfully fitted by the FN equation. With the thermionic emission model, the FE currents are clearly elucidated by the tunneling of thermal electrons through the contact barrier height of metallic carbon nanotubes and tip semiconductors, and through the conduction band of semiconducting tips to vacuum. The interface barrier height increases as temperatures increase. Although
Eq. (1) is valid only for semiconductors, the FE contribution from the armophous carbon caps play an essential role to the total emission of aligned metallic CNTs.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the National Science Council of the Republic of China under contract No. NSC 92-2112-M007-004 and the Ministry of Education under contract EP-92-FA04AA
